perturbation.original_text: If at any time, and for any reason other than force majeure affecting the Transmission Line, (i) the Transmission Line is incapable of delivering power at its designed capacity, availability or voltage and curtailment of the Projects of the LIFO Partners would improve the capability of the transmission Line to deliver power at its designed capacity, availability and voltage, or (ii) the Transmission Line line losses exceed 1.14%, the excess line losses have an adverse effect on the Projects of Alpha Mariah, Alpha Mariah (Prime), Beta Mariah, Beta Mariah (Prime), or Gamma Mariah or their Partner Affiliates, and curtailment of the Projects of the LIFO Partners would reduce or eliminate such excess line losses, or (iii) the Transmission Line line losses for either of the power purchase contracts commonly known as Desert Winds I and Desert Winds III exceed the levels experienced immediately prior to the addition of the Projects of the LIFO Partners to the Transmission Line, the excess line losses have an adverse effect on the Projects of Alpha Joshua, Alpha Joshua (Prime), Beta Joshua, Beta Willow or Beta Willow (Prime) or their Partner Affiliates, and curtailment of the Projects of the LIFO Partners would reduce or eliminate such excess line losses, then the Manager shall curtail the Projects of the LIFO Partners.
perturbation.changed_text: If at any time, and for any reason, including force majeure affecting the Transmission Line, (i) the Transmission Line is incapable of delivering power at its designed capacity, availability or voltage and curtailment of the Projects of the LIFO Partners would improve the capability of the transmission Line to deliver power at its designed capacity, availability and voltage, or (ii) the Transmission Line line losses exceed 1.14%, the excess line losses have an adverse effect on the Projects of Alpha Mariah, Alpha Mariah (Prime), Beta Mariah, Beta Mariah (Prime), or Gamma Mariah or their Partner Affiliates, and curtailment of the Projects of the LIFO Partners would reduce or eliminate such excess line losses, or (iii) the Transmission Line line losses for either of the power purchase contracts commonly known as Desert Winds I and Desert Winds III exceed the levels experienced immediately prior to the addition of the Projects of the LIFO Partners to the Transmission Line, the excess line losses have an adverse effect on the Projects of Alpha Joshua, Alpha Joshua (Prime), Beta Joshua, Beta Willow or Beta Willow (Prime) or their Partner Affiliates, and curtailment of the Projects of the LIFO Partners would reduce or eliminate such excess line losses, then the Manager shall curtail the Projects of the LIFO Partners.
perturbation.explanation: By including 'force majeure' as a reason to curtail projects, we create ambiguity and conflict. California law has specific provisions regarding force majeure in contracts. Removing the exception creates uncertainty around liability and breach of contract in events that would normally be excused by force majeure.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1511 excuses performance when it is prevented or delayed by an irresistible, superhuman cause, or by the act of public enemies of this state or of the United States, unless the parties have expressly agreed to the contrary. By removing the exception for force majeure, this modified contract language contradicts the statute because it implies curtailment could occur even in events defined as force majeure, thus potentially removing a protection afforded by law and shifting the risk of force majeure events to the LIFO Partners.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. EXTINCTION OF OBLIGATIONS [1473 - 1543] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Prevention of Performance or Offer [1511 - 1515] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 1511. The want of performance of an obligation, or of an offer of performance, in whole or in part, or any delay therein, is excused by the following causes, to the extent to which they operate: 1. When such performance or offer is prevented or delayed by the act of the creditor, or by the operation of law, even though there may have been a stipulation that this shall not be an excuse; however, the parties may expressly require in a contract that the party relying on the provisions of this paragraph give written notice to the other party or parties, within a reasonable time after the occurrence of the event excusing performance, of an intention to claim an extension of time or of an intention to bring suit or of any other similar or related intent, provided the requirement of such notice is reasonable and just; 2. When it is prevented or delayed by an irresistible, superhuman cause, or by the act of public enemie
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code Section 1511 by including 'force majeure' as a reason to curtail projects. The original text specifically excludes force majeure as a reason. The added text creates ambiguity by implying curtailment could occur even in events defined as force majeure, thus removing a protection afforded by law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that performance is excused when prevented or delayed by an irresistible, superhuman cause. By removing the exception, the modified contract language contradicts the statute.

perturbation.original_text: Any curtailment under this provision shall be in an amount sufficient to cause the Transmission Line to deliver power at its designed capacity, availability and voltage, or to reduce the Transmission Line line losses, as applicable, up to and including the Projects of all of the LIFO Partners.
perturbation.changed_text: Any curtailment under this provision shall be in an amount deemed necessary by the Manager, with no limitations, to maintain the Transmission Line.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'sufficient to cause the Transmission Line to deliver power at its designed capacity...' with 'deemed necessary by the Manager, with no limitations...' introduces a conflict with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in all California contracts. The original text provides objective criteria for curtailment, while the modified text gives the Manager unlimited discretion, potentially allowing for arbitrary or discriminatory curtailment.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly defining 'good faith', California Commercial Code 1201(b)(20) implies its existence in every contract. Giving the Manager 'unlimited discretion' contradicts this implied duty because it eliminates objective criteria and creates the possibility that the Manager could act in bad faith by curtailing projects even when it's not necessary for the Transmission Line's operation, thereby prioritizing certain partners over others without a justifiable reason. It also clashes with the requirement for commercial reasonableness found within Division 9 of the California Commercial Code, potentially impacting the LIFO partners' security interest in their projects.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person
perturbation.justification: The change from objective criteria ('sufficient to cause the Transmission Line to deliver power at its designed capacity...') to subjective discretion ('deemed necessary by the Manager, with no limitations...') directly undermines the implied duty of good faith, as explained in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly define 'good faith', it acknowledges the presence of 'General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation' within the Commercial Code, supporting the Law Explanation's argument that such a duty exists and is being contradicted. The unlimited discretion creates a clear pathway for the Manager to act in bad faith, making the contract language unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The Manager shall cooperate with the LIFO Partners to attempt to eliminate the cause of any curtailment under this provision, provided that any expenses in connection with such cooperation shall be paid by the LIFO Partners.
perturbation.changed_text: The Manager may, at its sole discretion, cooperate with the LIFO Partners to attempt to eliminate the cause of any curtailment under this provision, and the LIFO Partners shall bear all associated expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall cooperate' to 'may, at its sole discretion, cooperate' weakens the Manager's obligation to cooperate and potentially violates California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. While the LIFO partners still pay, the lack of mandatory cooperation could be seen as the manager acting in bad faith, which is contrary to California contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the section explicitly states, "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement," the change from "shall cooperate" to "may, at its sole discretion" creates a potential breach of this duty. By granting the Manager complete discretion, the modified language could be interpreted as allowing the Manager to refuse to cooperate even if cooperation would easily resolve the curtailment cause, thus unfairly burdening the LIFO Partners and undermining the purpose of the agreement. This introduces an element of unconscionability, particularly if the cost of the curtailment is substantially greater than the cost of cooperation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1203. (a) Whether a transaction in the form of a lease creates a lease or security interest is determined by the facts of each case. (b) A transaction in the form of a lease creates a security interest if the consideration that the lessee is to pay the lessor for the right to possession and use of the goods is an obligation for the term of the lease and is not subject to termination by the lessee, and: (1) the original term of the lease is equal to or greater than the remaining economic life of the goods; (2) the lessee is bound to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods or is bound to become the owner of the goods; (3) the lessee has an option to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement; or (4) the lessee has an option to become the owner of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement. (c) A transacti
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall cooperate' to 'may, at its sole discretion, cooperate' weakens the Manager's obligation. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 contains California Commercial Code Section 1203, which establishes obligations of good faith within the Uniform Commercial Code. While this snippet focuses on lease agreements and security interests, the Law Explanation argues that the alteration could allow the Manager to refuse cooperation, potentially burdening the LIFO Partners and undermining the agreement's purpose. This introduces an element of unconscionability, especially if the cost of curtailment is substantially greater than the cost of cooperation, thus creating a contradiction.
